---
label: "Multi-Agent Patterns"
icon: "🤖"
---

## Why Multiple Agents?

A single agent hits limits: context window constraints, tool overload (too many tools degrade performance), and the inability to hold conflicting perspectives simultaneously. Multi-agent systems solve this by specializing — each agent has a focused role, limited tools, and clear responsibilities.

<AnalogyBox emoji="🏢" title="Division of Labor at McKesson">
A single data steward can't handle all of MDM alone — you need specialists: one for supplier matching, one for material master data, one for regulatory compliance. Each has their own tools (SAP screens, D&B lookups, FDA databases) and domain expertise. Multi-agent AI follows the same principle: specialization through division of labor.
</AnalogyBox>

### Three Orchestration Patterns

<ComparisonTable title="Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns" columns={['Pattern','How It Works','Pros','Cons','Re³ Example']} rows={[
  ['Pipeline','Agent A → Agent B → Agent C (sequential)','Simple, predictable','No parallelism, single point of failure','Not used — too rigid'],
  ['Hierarchical','Supervisor delegates to worker agents','Clear control, organized','Supervisor is bottleneck, single vision','Forge (supervisor) → 5 debater agents'],
  ['Collaborative','Agents communicate as peers, negotiate','Diverse perspectives, emergent insight','Complex coordination, harder to debug','Re³ debate rounds — agents argue as peers'],
]}/>

### The Supervisor Pattern in LangGraph

<CodeBlock b64="" />

### Graph Assembly

<CodeBlock b64="" />

Notice the hub-and-spoke pattern: every worker reports back to the supervisor, who decides what happens next. This is the same pattern as Forge in Re³.

<SeeItInRe3 text="Re³ implements the hierarchical pattern: Forge (supervisor) selects 5 debater agents from 25, then agents argue as peers (collaborative pattern), and Atlas moderates quality (another supervisor layer)." />

<Quiz
  question="You're building a multi-agent system for supplier due diligence at McKesson. Which orchestration pattern best fits: one agent checks sanctions lists, one verifies financial health, one reviews compliance history, and a human steward makes the final decision?"
  options={["Pipeline — sanctions → financial → compliance → human", "Hierarchical — supervisor delegates to specialist agents, then routes to human", "Collaborative — all agents discuss the supplier simultaneously", "Single agent with multiple tools"]}
  correctIndex={1}
  explanation="Hierarchical fits best: a supervisor can delegate checks in parallel to specialists, aggregate their findings, and route to human review only when needed. Pipeline is too rigid (what if sanctions check needs re-running after financial data arrives?). Collaborative adds complexity without benefit since the checks are independent."
/>
